topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara slammed the laptop shut with a sharp click that echoed in the otherwise silent office. She stared at the screen, her jaw tight, the muscles in her neck visibly straining. The lines of code, the meticulously organized project files—they were all exactly the same. She leaned back in her chair, rubbing the bridge of her nose with two fingers, a slow, deliberate motion that spoke volumes of her mounting frustration. "This is impossible," she muttered to the empty room, the sound thin and brittle. She scrolled down, comparing the version history logs, each entry mirroring the other’s with unsettling precision. A prickle of something sharp began behind her eyes. She stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between her desk and the filing cabinet, her steps quick and jerky. The sheer duplication of effort made her breath catch, a shallow, ragged intake of air.

[story 2]

Mark sighed, a long, drawn-out exhalation that seemed to carry the weight of a thousand unresolved issues. He picked up his coffee mug, turning it slowly in his hands until the ceramic felt cold against his palms. His gaze flicked between his monitor and the physical blueprints spread across the table—identical layouts, identical annotations. A low hum of irritation vibrated beneath his skin, making his shoulders tense up near his ears. He tapped a pen against the edge of the schematic, a rapid, uneven rhythm. "Seriously?" he questioned aloud, his voice pitched low, edged with disbelief. He leaned in closer, squinting at the shared document, his brow furrowing so deeply it seemed etched into his skin. Every identical section sent a wave of vexation washing over him, making him want to crumple the paper just to feel something different.

[story 3]

"Look at this," Chloe said, her voice tight, barely above a whisper as she gestured toward the dual prototypes resting on the workbench. Her hands clenched into fists at her sides, the knuckles turning white under the fluorescent lights. She took a deep, shaky breath, trying to steady the tremor in her hands. The symmetry of the components was glaring, offensive in its perfect replication. She walked around the table twice, her heels clicking sharply on the linoleum floor, a restless energy radiating off her. She stopped in front of the first unit, tracing the seam with a fingertip, then moved to the second, her expression hardening into a mask of profound displeasure. She let out a short, sharp sound—a frustrated puff of air—before turning back to the display, her posture rigid with suppressed agitation.
